transcript.text: ...deal of talk and speculation, how much of it may be extra-terrestrial, and how much of it may be a terrestrial base.
Hmm, like we probably kept going back there?
Well, there is some speculation to say that we have the technology and the capabilities of going back there,
And there are other countries that have talked and devoted sections of their, you know, gross national product to set up facilities on the moon and to explore the mining potential.
So, it's not a far-fetched concept, but actually, that in the end of the world would provide a great deal of raw essential material,
And perhaps we could give our own planet a little bit of a break here.
If we're going back, why are we keeping it a secret when we kept the first groupings of Apollo missions so public?
Well, that particular program, of course, was the inspiration of President Kennedy when he challenged us to put forward a progressive space program,
And that it would unite, and not only people in their thinking and education and technology, but unite economies as well,
To give an opposite direction towards the same old status quo, so to speak.
And so that program and those programs were, of course, very visible because the administration's honor sort of rested upon the continuance.
Those were the good things, and obviously, as we were regulated, we needed key things for a change,
And so those programs continued to be very much in the forefront.
Then, of course, after Apollo 17 and the 1970s, there were so many budget cuts,
There was a great amount of questioning of why we would have put money to go into space,
When obviously we needed a great deal of funds to fix problems here on this planet.
And so basically, for lack of anything better, public support simply evaporated.
And then it was very easy, too, to cut these programs and completely throw it off.
Do you think they're black ops trips?
I would not be surprised if they're black ops trips,
Because, you know, naturally we are experimenting.
Nothing ever stands still.
When we look at the development of our research and development of skunk works and things like that,
The public generally gets sight of these things, you know, one decade or sometimes two decades after the development itself.
And nothing ever stands still.
We never completely kill any program.
We never, you know, whitewash it.
There's always something going on behind the scenes.
And I would not, for a minute, doubt that the benefits of going to the moon were very obvious
And that we have to continue to go there.
How would they launch without the public?
Well, there's a different way to look at that.
Of course, there are other launch facilities that are available in the Indian Ocean.
And so, you know, you launch from that particular facility and you can be in equatorial orbit,
You know, way for a long time to get to the west coast.
Or, of course, there have been others who have suggested that we have progressed past the fiery vehicle
To a missile technology and a zealous technology that does not require to sit on the back of this iron five.
What kind of materials, if indeed it is us, what kind of materials do you think they're mining from the moon?
Well, there's a number of...
I forgot to do the chest fly.
Well, I said to do the shoulder fly.
This is where the aids come in handy.
Maybe they're too light, though.

As far as things like titanium gold, diamonds, industrial composites that we use, and so on and so forth,
Is much more readily available and much more abundant on that satellite body.

I don't think there's carbon.
Well, that's a debatable point.
I mean, obviously...
Rekino for the yoga.
...the body is developed from the same material the planet is developed from in one stage or another.

And after all, diamond...
For all purposes, it's a very common stone.
If it would not be held back by the diamond consortium, you could buy it for a dollar carat.
So it's more of intrinsic value than it really is a substance.

Official knowledge of NASA, because here we are struggling with a space shuttle...
